title: Great place to work
pros: Honestly, being at Cisco is a total breath of fresh air compared to the usual "grind culture" in tech. What really sets it apart for me is that they actually treat you like a human being with a life outside of Slack. Between the "Days for Me" (which are basically free long weekends the whole company takes together) and the super flexible hybrid setup, you never feel burnt out. The coolest part, though, is the sheer scale of what weâre working on. One day youâre looking at AI-driven networking and the next youâre seeing how the Splunk integration is changing the game for security. Itâs a massive portfolio, so youâre never "stuck" in one niche.
cons: If thereâs a downside, it might be that the portfolio is absolutely massive. Sometimes it feels like youâre trying to learn a library thatâs being written while you're reading it. Between the constant acquisitions (like Splunk) and the shift into AI-native networking, it can be legitimately overwhelming to keep up with everything. But you can turn all of these into opportunities.

title: Great fit for the right person
body: Great fit for the right person
pros: Cisco has a lot of experienced, highly capable people with deep institutional knowledge. Youâre surrounded by teammates and leaders who have seen a lot, and thereâs a real opportunity to learn best practices in strategy, execution, and cross-functional collaboration. The company also provides a stable environment, strong brand, and generally supportive teams.
cons: The biggest downside is the amount of red tape. Decision-making can be slow, processes are heavy, and it often takes multiple layers of approvals to get work moving. Even when thereâs alignment on the âwhat,â execution can drag due to internal complexity and coordination overhead.

title: Great compensation, Cross-functional collaboration very political, Frequent layoffs
body: Great compensation, Cross-functional collaboration very political, Frequent layoffs
pros: * Great compensation package * Effectively repositioning themselves to take advantage of AI market (especially in security)
cons: * Company built from multiple technical acquisitions over time, which leads to a confusing mix of internal technologies for employees and overwhelming set of product offerings for customers and the sales force * Upper-level management unable to agree on a focused set of goals for internal systems * Lots of internal politics, without a clear model for cross-functional collaboration * Too many levels of the organization means that department annual goals aren't set until the middle of Q2, leaving ICs to scramble to deliver on commitments

title: Ups & Downs, Decent Pay, When's the Next LR
body: Ups & Downs, Decent Pay, When's the Next LR
pros: Depends on your team, level of experience, tolerance for redundant/ever changing leadership, your individual manager, and how much wondering if you'll be impacted by the next LR bothers you. This can be a great place, there is money to be made/opportunities for advancement, but as with anywhere not all managers are created equal, which of course makes a huge difference in your experience. Don't expect smooth sailing for extended periods.
cons: Constant Leadership changes, Redundant leadership, depending on the manager they can be amazing or unfortunately they can be awful and fall into the stereotypes you see across the tech space. LRs constantly, even after they say "we cut deep enough this time so we don't have to put everyone through this again" just to have another 5 months later.

title: Contractor pay is embarrassingly low for amount of work
body: Contractor pay is embarrassingly low for amount of work
pros: Fully remote. Managers literally don't care where you are located or where you take calls from.
cons: They hire as contractors through recruitment companies and there is zero consistency in salary. They say up front that they don't guarantee 40 hours, but the truth is they don't even guarantee 30 hours, despite it being a full time position. That said, they expect you to be available 8 hrs/day. You are not allowed to bill any time to Cisco. All time is billed to the project you're on, so if you have no time left on the project to bill against, you don't get paid for that time. They mandate that you take a "day for me" a few times per year unpaid and the last two weeks of the year are mandated off days unpaid.

title: Retirement Health insurance benefit - scam
pros: Can't think of any and I have tried.
cons: in my opinions the Retirement Health insurance benefit is a scam. The overall health benefit is self funded by CISCO. Retires pay 3x the going rate as normal market rates. The company can set the rate an anything it wants as it is self funded. It just shows large subsidy to current employees so it looks appealing to current employees. I would guess that any retire that opts for this plan is actual helping to subsiding the plan. Current rates for a retired family is ~$6000 per month = $72,000 a year plus a $3000 deductible totaling $75,000. However you still pay 20% co-insurance on care after the deductible. So $75,000 + for health care per person at Cisco.... REALLY!!! To put that in perspective if it was that cost for all its current employees that is over $6.5 BILLION a year!!!!

title: Decent place to work, depending on your politics
body: Decent place to work, depending on your politics
pros: Extra paid days off beyond normal holidays (e.g. 'A day for me' several times a year) Good pay
cons: Heavily investing in politics, and enforcement of political messaging. It is not uncommon to have mandatory company meetings to listen to speeches from family members of elected officials. (Liberal/left only) Ostensibly open to all ideas, as long as they don't conflict with their preferred ideas. They encourage speech that is an 'echo' of their preferred messaging, but I saw several immediate terminations (like 1 hour later) after someone spoke out an opposite opinion during a time that opinions were 'welcomed' (again as long as it aligned with the orthodoxy) If your politics align with the US political left, you will be loved and fit right in here.

title: Rampant survivor culture issues, leading to competition within

pros: I was expected to build a new product, and the focus was on that business. The customer base is significant, and resources are plentiful Strong networking experience
cons: I heard from employees that Cisco attempted to build a cloud but failed multiple times. The cloud they have is stitched together from their own products, but there is no capacity planning, it is not at all elastic, and ran out of IP addresses, and need a month to add more servers Customers are unhappy, product deadlines are imposed from the top, and bureaucracy consumes time, expands teams, and becomes entrenched. The new leadership can talk but can't walk. They recently spent $20B to buy Splunk, which didn't transition to the cloud, nor did Ciscoâit's going to be a disaster.

title: Impressive Engineering, Troubling Cultural Blind Spots
body: Impressive Engineering, Troubling Cultural Blind Spots
pros: Overall, Cisco is a good place to work. As a senior systems engineer, I had the opportunity to collaborate with many thoughtful, capable, and genuine people across teamsâfrom engineering and test to compliance, product management, executives, manufacturing, and TAC. The company encourages technical excellence, and there are pockets of genuinely supportive leadership. That said, the experience can vary dramatically depending on your org and manager, and some managers operate with impunity despite repeated and serious concerns.
cons: If you end up with a manager who manipulates systems and relationshipsâundermining direct reports, peers, and even superiors to secure promotion or survivalâthe only real option is to leave. Donât expect HR, ER, or internal processes to intervene meaningfully, even when misconduct is extensively documented and corroborated by multiple witnesses. This toxic behavior often goes unpunished and actively rewardedâwith visibility, advancement, incentives, and plenty of opportunities to retaliate against those who speak up. Cisco has trimmed HR/ER and internal enforcement functions so much that the cultural messaging from the Cisco People Office feels performative and aspirational rather than reflective of ground-level realities.

title: Strong Brand, Smart Teams, But Navigating Change Can Be Challenging
body: Strong Brand, Smart Teams, But Navigating Change Can Be Challenging
pros: Prestigious Global Brand: Working at Cisco offers strong name recognition and credibility on your resume. Smart, Collaborative Teams: Surrounded by talented professionals who are often willing to share knowledge and support cross-functional collaboration. Flexible Work Environment: Hybrid/remote work culture with a focus on results over face time, depending on the team. Great Benefits: Comprehensive healthcare, generous PTO, 401(k) match, employee stock purchase plan, and wellness programs. Diversity & Inclusion Efforts: Cisco invests in employee resource groups and DEI initiatives, making it a welcoming environment for many. Opportunities for Internal Mobility: Easier to explore lateral moves across functions and teams if you network internally. Access to Tools & Resources: Top-tier platforms and internal tools to help with data, automation, and process improvement.
cons: Frequent Restructuring: Ongoing reorgs can create uncertainty, limit long-term planning, and cause disruption in team dynamics. Limited Promotion Path in Operations: Roles in Partner/Channel Operations may not have as clear or fast a path to leadership as Sales or Engineering. Bureaucracy & Siloed Teams: Large company dynamics can slow down decision-making and cross-functional execution. Inconsistent Leadership Styles: Experience can vary widely depending on your direct manager and org alignment. Visibility Can Be a Challenge: Ops roles supporting Sales may be under-recognized despite their strategic impact. Pressure During QBRs/FY Planning: especially during sales cycles and end-of-quarter crunches.

title: Great company but also has its drawbacks
body: Great company but also has its drawbacks
pros: I was fortunate to work there over 20 years doing both inside sales and field sales. There are many competitive benefits, and they pay well. The people in Cisco are mostly exceptional to work with and a highlight of working there.
cons: Some roles became overwhelming as each year you were expected to do more with less resources. Key sales resources would be taken away or there'd be hiring freezes and you'd have to pick up the slack and were expected to overachieve on your sales goal. Cisco could do a better job making information about employee development programs known as they have various programs, but sometimes you just find out randomly through word of mouth. Even if someone needs to be nominated for a program, the employees should know they exist.

title: Once a great company...

cons: Moving to the cloud has been an ongoing challenge. Perhaps the Splunk acquisition will help, but having hardware people try and lead this transition over the pass 10+ years has not benefited the company nor the stock price.

title: The times they are a changing.....
body: The times they are a changing.....
pros: Good pay and benefits. Lots of smart, hard working, driven co-workers. Challenging projects to work on. 80 hours of time to give per year (volunteer time), unlimited PTO, 6 days for me annually, birthday off, 10+ holidays per year in United States.
cons: Lay-offs continue to impact morale, and most teams are short-staffed and working long hours. The executive level politics are skilling the company. Lots of exec level turn-over . There is a clear feeling that the execs SAY they care about employees, but their actions speak more loudly that they do NOT care. The work-life balance is suffering for many.

title: Excellent Workplace with the right manager
body: Excellent Workplace with the right manager
pros: - CISCO as a company provides a great environment for balancing work and personal life. - The pay is generally competitive from what I have seen elsewhere. - The technical support for issues such as laptop problems is quite good. - Met a lot of great people at CISCO
cons: - If you have a great manager, it's heaven. If you have a jerk of a manager, then it's a nightmare. I've had both. - There is always organizational politics that can destroy the morale of any company's employees, and CISCO is not any different.

body: Culture is rapidly changing before our eyes
pros: - Highly collaborative environment - Top notch work life balance (many teams commit to 100% remote) - Compensation and benefits are generous

title: Increasingly toxic workplace in the world of tech
body: Increasingly toxic workplace in the world of tech
pros: Good salary and benefits. You might get a good manager for a short time, but they will inevitably leave or get pushed out.
cons: Blatantly discriminate against older (e.g., over 40 years old) workers. Lots of churn and re-orgs. If you join a non-toxic group with a good manager - that could change in a matter of months. Lots of tribalism and fiefdoms - good luck if your role is trying to get different groups or even different BUs to collaborate - most Ciscoians have blinders on and can't see what's best for the company as a whole nor their customers. Got rid of accumulated PTO. Constant layoffs that are not performance related. You can get an exemplary performance review and huge bonus one year and get laid off the next with the same level of performance and output.

title: Interesting Work, Average Interview, High Layoff
body: Interesting Work, Average Interview, High Layoff
pros: The pay, work, and remote flexibility are very appropriate and there are many benefits included in compensation for software engineers. The work itself is also challenging, but rewarding if you like cloud/network engineering.
cons: If you have followed the news over the last year, you probably saw that Cisco has had many layoffs in the last 12 months. At least 3 that I am aware of. New hires are often chosen for this because they are largely more replaceable than engineers that have been there for 10+ years and are local experts that would be very hard to replace. So I would advise against hiring into this company until its internal business issues have settled out. Work/life balance is also very hit or miss, but thats a lot of tech these days.

title: Awesome people, ignorant executive leadership
pros: - Great benefits - Mostly competitive pay - Decent opportunity for movement - Fully remote - Fantastic individual contributor culture
cons: Small stuff: - Lots of meetings, but that's just remote tech life - An already silo'd company feels extra silo'd when we are basically all remote - Projects might not truly see the light of day, but that's the cold corporate world for you - Can be hard to get converted from a contractor to a full-time employee Big stuff: - Executive leadership is ignorant of how people lower down the totem pole work. Or worse yet, they just don't care. Recent layoffs that hit the part of the company that had been meeting projections are laughable. All in the name of "optimizing for growth around AI" - Layoffs are happening twice a year at this point. TWICE. And they aren't small either. The "always be hiring, always be firing" method feels terrifying to work under. - Cisco is a dinosaur (old) company. I think their focus on AI will lead them nowhere since there's no way they can keep up with the more modern, agile tech companies.

title: good work life balance but always worried about getting laid off
body: good work life balance but always worried about getting laid off
pros: work life balance is usually great. (we'll see after a 10% redux in headcount this year though), good benefits, empathetic management.
cons: feels like nothing gets done with the research sometimes. as a company not very innovative in the field. pay below average. and LAYOFFS. I've been here just over a year and there have been 3 rounds of mass layoffs, disproportionately impacting junior people with senior people expected to absorb all that work. Layoffs always announced in stressful ways too-- most recently it was leaked to the press and then we were told by leadership "oops yeah that's true, you'll find out if you are laid off on [date in six weeks]".

title: Will layoff every 6 months. No plan to return to growth. Now a kinder, gentler Computer Associates.
body: Will layoff every 6 months. No plan to return to growth. Now a kinder, gentler Computer Associates.
pros: Employee culture at the individual contributor level is fantastic. Work/Life Balance is good.
cons: Back to laying off every 6 months. The most likely reason you will get laid off is "this year's executive" for your function wants to do things differently. Or your team is marooned in the wrong organization. How did your team get marooned? From last year's executive's re-org. Being re-orged into IT or Operations is a common death sentence and you should begin a 6 month exit plan as soon as it happens. Leadership couldn't answer a "How to return to growth?" question after the most recent layoff. CEO had a "hope in 6 months" answer. Services EVP had a word salad. This was a seeded question in a moderated forum. Newly appointed Chief Product Officer is coming from a lackluster division (WebEx) and admitted to knowing nothing about Enterprise Networking. At this point, Cisco cannot attract innovative leaders or technical talent to reverse downward spiral.

title: Great culture
pros: Great culture with great people. Competitive pay and benefits
cons: Frequent layoffs and restructures in my time at Cisco

title: Lost its way?
pros: When times are good, the culture and benefits are outstanding
cons: I worked on 3 projects in less than a year due to reorganizations that moved a bunch of people around, and then I was hit in the layoffs that affected 7% of the company.

title: Great people and company culture
pros: Awesome company culture and generally nice, hard working employees
cons: Employees suffer for management failures

title: Fiddling while Rome burns
pros: The initial compensation packages are generally good (though stock grants have been poor and you will have to leave the company to get a sizable pay raise).
cons: It's a political nightmare mixed with a lot of DEI nonsense. Their glory years are behind them and all their major product lines are under attack by strong competitors. Some senior management is trying to change the company culture and fight back in the market by pushing Meraki to the front of the product line, but Meraki by itself is not an answer. The problems are deep and structural and they require knowledgeable, fearless leaders to make any progress. Unfortunately for Cisco, those are few and far between. I watched several good people try to make progress on substantial issues, all to have them generally fail. Most VPs have a relatively cushy job and are just milking the compensation package while Rome burns.
